sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
> **Note:** To learn about best practices for auditing the actions of temporary
> database users, see [Audit Temporary Database Users](audit-temp-db-users).

Use the following procedure to set up database auditing:

By default, Atlas logs the failed authentication attempts of both
known and unknown users in the audit log of the [primary](manual/reference/glossary) node.

Alternatively, click Use Custom JSON Filter to manually
enter an [audit filter](manual/tutorial/configure-audit-filters)
as a JSON string. For more information on configuring custom audit
filters in Atlas, see [Configure a Custom Auditing Filter](auditing-custom-filter).

> **Note:** Deselecting the `authenticate` action prevents Atlas from
> auditing authentication failures.

> **Note:** When selecting the [authorization success granularity](manual/reference/parameters)
> of auditing for the `authCheck` event action, Atlas does
> not support different selections for reads and writes. For example,
> you may not select Successes and Failures for authCheck Reads
> and Failures for authCheck Writes. If you
> select both authCheck Reads and authCheck Writes,
> Atlas automatically applies your selected granularity to both.

To retrieve the audit logs in Atlas, see [log data](mongodb-logs). To retrieve the audit logs using the API, see
[Logs](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-downloadgroupclusterlog).

sections.heading: Configure a Custom Auditing Filter
sections.body:
> **Note:** This feature is not available for any of the following deployments:
>
> - Free clusters
>
> - Flex clusters
>
> To learn more, see [Limits](limits).

Atlas supports specifying a JSON-formatted audit filter
for customizing [MongoDB Auditing](manual/core/auditing).

Custom audit filters let users forgo the managed
Atlas UI auditing filter builder
in favor of hand-tailored granular control of event auditing.
Atlas checks only that the custom filter uses valid
JSON syntax, and doesn't validate or test the filter's functionality.

The audit filter document must resolve to a query that matches one or
more fields in the [audit event message](manual/reference/audit-message).
The filter document can use combinations of [query operators](manual/reference/mql/query-predicates) and equality
conditions to match the desired audit messages.

To view example auditing filters, see
[Example Auditing Filters](auditing-filter-example). To learn more about configuring MongoDB
auditing filters, see [Configure Audit Filter](manual/tutorial/configure-audit-filters).

> **Important:** Atlas uses a [Atlas High Availability](high-availability) strategy
> for enabling or updating audit configuration settings across all
> clusters in the Atlas project. Rolling updates require at least
> one election per replica set.
>
> To learn more about testing application resilience to replica set
> elections, see
> Test Primary Failover. To learn more
> about how Atlas provides high availability, see
> [Atlas High Availability](high-availability).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
> **Warning:** Enabling Audit authorization successes can severely
> impact cluster performance. Enable this option with caution.

For audit filters specifying the
`authCheck` [action type](manual/reference/audit-message),
the auditing system logs only authorization failures for
any specified `param.command` by default. Enabling
Audit authorization successes directs the auditing
system to also log authorization successes. If you don't enable
[auditAuthorizationSuccess](manual/reference/parameters), your
audit system can't log successful CRUD operations specified in the
[action type](manual/reference/audit-message).
To learn more,
see [auditAuthorizationSuccess](manual/reference/parameters).

sections.heading: Edit a Custom Auditing Filter
sections.body: You can edit your filter at any time:

sections.heading: Step 1: Edit the filter.
sections.body:
- Under **Database Auditing** arrow-right
  **Configure Your Auditing Filter**, click
  **Use Custom JSON Filter**.

- Make the required changes.

- Click **Save**.

sections.heading: Step 1: View your filter.
sections.body:
Your custom auditing filter displays under
**Database Auditing**.

sections.heading: Example Auditing Filters
sections.body:
Use the following example auditing filters for guidance in constructing
your own filters.

> **Important:** These examples are not intended for use in
> production environments, nor are they a replacement for familiarity
> with the [MongoDB Auditing Documentation](manual/core/auditing).

sections.heading: Audit all authentication events for known users
Audit all authentication events for known users and authentication failures for unknown users
Audit authentication events for the "myClusterAdministrator" user
Audit unauthorized attempts at executing the selected commands
Manage Database Auditing with the Atlas CLI
sections.body:
You can use the Atlas CLI to manage database auditing configuration
for your projects.

sections.heading: Update Auditing Configuration
sections.body:
To update the auditing configuration for the specified project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas auditing update [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas auditing update](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-auditing-update).

sections.heading: View Auditing Configuration
sections.body:
To return the auditing configuration for the specified project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas auditing describe [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas auditing describe](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-auditing-describe).
